It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Starting level 1. The board appears to have a split background (yellow left, orange right) with a large black shape in the center and two blue squares near the bottom. Need to understand the structure and goal.
- Plan: First inspect the segmentation to understand objects, then probe actions to understand mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 56 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

The passage is at cols 29-33. The squares can't move into the passage because the passage is between the left and right corridors.

Looking at the history more carefully:
- Entry 8: RIGHT → left square at cols 19-23, right square at cols 39-43
- Entry 9: RIGHT → left square at cols 19-23 (no change), right square at cols 34-38
So when I pressed RIGHT, the right square moved from cols 39-43 to cols 34-38 (towards the passage). But the left square stayed at cols 19-23. This suggests that RIGHT moves the squares towards the passage (left square moves right, right square moves left).
Wait, that doesn't make sense. Let me re-examine.
- Entry 7: LEFT → left square at cols 14-18, right square at cols 44-48
- Entry 8: RIGHT → left square at cols 19-23, right square at cols 39-43
So LEFT moves the left square further left (cols 14-18) and the right square further right (cols 44-48). RIGHT moves the left square towards the passage (cols 19-23) and the right square towards the passage (cols 39-43).
So LEFT/RIGHT moves the squares away/towards the passage. The left square can be at cols 14, 19, or 24. The right square can be at cols 34, 39, or 44.
Currently, the squares are at cols 24-28 (left) and 34-38 (right), which is the rightmost position for the left square and the leftmost position for the right square. This means the squares are as close to the passage as possible.
Now, what about UP/DOWN? Looking at the history:

- Entry 19: RIGHT → left square at rows 49-53, right square at rows 54-58
- Entry 24: RIGHT → left square at rows 39-43, right square at rows 44-48
So the squares can move to different rows. The rows seem to be 39-43, 44-48, 49-53, 54-58, etc.

- Entry 16: RIGHT → left square at rows 44-48, right square at rows 49-53 (no change in rows)

Actually, I think the key insight is that the squares can move to specific positions, and the goal might be to get them to a specific configuration. Let me think about what configuration would make sense.
Looking at the black shape, it has a complex boundary with corridors. The squares are in the corridors. The passage is between the corridors. Maybe the goal is to get the squares to the passage and merge them?
But the squares can't move into the passage because the passage is only 5 cells wide, and the squares are 5x5. If both squares try to move into the passage, they would overlap.
Wait, maybe the goal is to get the squares to specific positions and then press SPACE to complete the level. Let me try pressing SPACE when the squares are at specific positions.
